learning_outcomes:
Economics 1. Major
Programme: Graduates of this programme will
achieve the following Knowledge, Skills, and Values and Attitudes
Outcomes. Knowledge Outcomes Knowledge of basic economic
theory and real-world economic issues including: (1) Using powerful analytical tools to
interpret complex economic phenomena; (2) issues related to Greater-China and
the world economy in an age of globalization, with an appreciation of the
power and limitations of theory; Skills Outcomes Competence in basic statistics,
econometrics, and mathematics, including: (1) the ability to apply empirical and
mathematical tools in testing economic theory and analyzing real-world
economic issues, and to make informed judgments on controversial economic
issues and policy debates; and (2) the ability to understand and predict
emerging economic trends in an age of globalization so as to facilitate
career planning and the management of personal, corporate, and public
finance in response to challenges from a fast-changing world. Competence in a set of generic
skills essential to the modern age, including: (1) the ability to articulate ideas in
verbal and written form; (2) bilingual proficiency in meeting the
challenges of globalization and Hong Kong as a gateway to China; (3) logical and critical thinking as a foundation
for life-long learning and future professional development; and (4) interpersonal communications and
collaboration skills. Values and Attitudes Outcomes
A set of personal attributes
conducive to good work and personal ethics for the 21st century, including: (1) honesty and integrity, with emphasis
on respecting intellectual property rights; (2) cross-cultural sensitivity and
tolerance as personal attributes needed to thrive in an increasingly
internationalized workforce; (3) the ability to engage in rational
discussions in an increasingly transparent and participatory society; and (4) embracing the philosophy of life-long
learning. 2. Minor
Programme: Upon the completion of the Minor
Programme in Economics, students will have demonstrated basic understanding
of fundamental issues related to economics such as: l fundamental theories that explain market behaviour and
changes in economic variables at the micro and macro levels, l the scientific approach of testing theories using
empirical data, and l the sensitivity to emerging economic trends that affect
career planning and the management of personal and corporate finance in a
fast-changing world. Students will have acquired knowledge
of basic aspects of economics with sufficient exposure and at a suitable
level to allow further study in economics-related subjects or to pursue a
related career.

Economics Economics Applicable
to students admitted in 2024-25
Major Programme Requirement
Students are required to complete a minimum of 72
units of courses as follows:
Units

and any two courses, offered by two different

1120, 1150, 1500, DSPS1001, 1003, 1004, GLSD1001, GPAD1020, 1076, 1077,
GRMD1302, 1401, 1402, PSYC1000, 1630, SOCI1001, 1201, SOSC1001, 1002,
1003, SOWK1001, 1113, 1114, URSP1001, 1002

ECON1101[a], 1111, 1902, 2021, 2121, 2901, 3011,
3021, 3121, 4901 and 4903 (capstone course)

level

For students taking two concentration areas, the

addition to the courses listed above, the Department may approve the
inclusion of relevant selected topics courses or courses at 5000 or above
level in the concentration areas.
Explanatory Notes:
1.  |  ECON courses at 2000 and
above level will be included in the calculation of Major GPA for honours
classification.
[a]  |  Students who have obtained grade 3 or above in both the
compulsory part and Module 1 (Calculus and Statistics) of the HKDSE
Mathematics examination may, upon application, be exempted from ECON1101,
in which case the Major Programme Requirement will be reduced by 2
units. Students who have obtained grade 3 or above in both
the compulsory part and Module 2 (Algebra and Calculus) of the HKDSE
Mathematics examination are not required to take ECON1101. The Major Programme
Requirement for these students will be reduced by 2 units. Students who have obtained (i) grade B or above in
GCE Mathematics (AL) and grade B or above in GCE Further Mathematics (AL)
or (ii) grade B or above in GCE Mathematics (AL) and grade B or above in
GCE Pure Mathematics (AL) are not required to take
ECON1101. The Major Programme Requirement for these students will be
reduced by 2 units.
[b]  |  Students are allowed to take ECON courses at 5000 or
above level to fulfill the Elective Courses requirement, subject to the
consent of the course instructor.
[c]  |  Students may declare a concentration area (i) after
the requisite courses for the concentration area have been completed, or
(ii) in the same term that the requisite courses are expected to be
completed. Upon completion of all requisite courses for a concentration
area, such concentration area will be indicated on academic transcripts.
[d]  |  Students are required to declare which MATH course is
to be counted towards the requirement of the Economic Theory concentration
area at their final term of attendance.

Explanatory Notes:

maximum will need to seek approval from the Registrar.
[c]  |  The recommended course pattern of College GE courses
varies slightly depending on student’s college affiliation.
[d]  |  In addition to ECON1101, 1111 and 2121, students are
required to take MATH1550 to fulfil 10 units of Mathematics requirements of
the Bachelor of Economics Programme at THU. In this connection, students
need to take a total of 108-109 units (instead of 105-106 units) at CUHK.
[e]  |  Courses 30510763 (Intermediate Macroeconomics) and
30510973 (Econometrics (1)) taken at THU can be transferred to fulfil the
requirements of ECON3021 and 3121.
[f]  |  Course 40510620 (Diploma Project (Thesis)) taken at THU
can be transferred to fulfil the requirements of ECON4901 and 4903.
[g]  |  Courses 12090052 (Military Theory, 2 units) and
12090062 (Military Skills, 2 units) are optional for Taiwan students.
Taiwan students who do not take these two courses can take another course 12530033
(Freshman Orientation and Training, 3 units) at THU.
[h]  |  Based on the design of the DDP, students can choose to
take their final CUHK term in the 1st term or 2nd term of the fourth year.
[i]  |  Students are required to complete at least 48 units (out
of 72) of major courses.
